label: ML Lifecycle — Gap Coverage
topic: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML

questions.stem: What is the purpose of exploratory data analysis (EDA) in the data preparation phase?

options.text: To quickly gain a deeper understanding of data using visualization tools before preparing it for model building
To deploy the model to production
To train the model on the raw data
To delete unnecessary AWS resources

explanation.correct: EDA with visualization tools helps quickly gain a deeper understanding of data characteristics, distributions, and relationships before proceeding with data wrangling and model building.

distractors.B: Deployment is a later pipeline stage. EDA is an early data exploration activity.
distractors.C: Training comes after data preparation. EDA is part of understanding the data before preparing it.
distractors.D: Resource cleanup is operational. EDA is analytical exploration of your dataset.

questions.stem: What is a recipe in AWS Glue DataBrew?

options.text: A saved set of transformation steps that can be updated, reused with other datasets, and deployed on a continuing basis
A cooking instruction set for generating food-related content
A model training configuration file
An IAM policy for data access

explanation.correct: In DataBrew, you can save transformation steps in a recipe, which can be updated or reused later with other datasets and deployed on a continuing basis for repeatable data preparation.

distractors.B: Recipes in DataBrew are data transformation steps, not cooking instructions.
distractors.C: Training configurations are separate from DataBrew recipes. Recipes handle data preparation transformations.
distractors.D: IAM policies manage access. DataBrew recipes are reusable data transformation workflows.

questions.stem: When collecting training data, what must you determine about the data ingestion method?

options.text: Whether the data is streaming (real-time continuous flow) or whether it can be loaded in a batch process
Whether the data is stored in a single AWS Region
Whether the data is larger than 1 TB
Whether the data was created in the last 24 hours

explanation.correct: When collecting data, you need to know if it's streaming data (continuous flow requiring real-time ingestion) or if you can load it in a batch process. AWS Glue can handle both, including from streaming services like Amazon MSK and Kinesis.

distractors.B: Region is an infrastructure concern. The ingestion method (streaming vs batch) determines your collection architecture.
distractors.C: Size affects storage and compute requirements but doesn't determine whether you use streaming or batch ingestion.
distractors.D: Data freshness matters but the key decision is the ingestion method — continuous stream or periodic batch.

questions.stem: What is Amazon SageMaker Model Registry?

options.text: A centralized repository for trained models and their history, used for versioning and tracking model lineage
A domain name registration service for ML endpoints
A data labeling service for training datasets
A compute instance type selector for training jobs

explanation.correct: SageMaker Model Registry is a centralized repository for trained models and their history. It works alongside Feature Store (for feature definitions) and Pipelines (for orchestration) in the MLOps ecosystem.

distractors.B: Domain registration is handled by Route 53. Model Registry stores and versions trained models.
distractors.C: Data labeling is Ground Truth's function. Model Registry manages trained model versions.
distractors.D: Instance selection is done during training job configuration. Model Registry stores completed model versions.

questions.stem: What happens when you increase the decision threshold for a binary classification model?

options.text: Fewer false positives occur, but more false negatives — the model becomes more conservative about predicting positive
Both false positives and false negatives decrease simultaneously
The model's accuracy always improves
The model stops making any predictions

explanation.correct: Increasing the threshold results in fewer false positives (model is more certain before predicting positive) but more false negatives (some actual positives don't meet the higher bar). It's a tradeoff.

distractors.B: You can't reduce both simultaneously. Increasing threshold reduces FP but increases FN — they trade off.
distractors.C: Accuracy may or may not improve. The threshold shifts the balance between error types.
distractors.D: The model still makes predictions. It just requires higher confidence before classifying as positive.

questions.stem: What do the false positive rate and true negative rate measure? (Select TWO)

options.text: False positive rate — the ratio of false positives to the sum of false positives and true negatives (how many non-positive items were incorrectly classified as positive)
True negative rate — the ratio of true negatives to the sum of false positives and true negatives (how many non-positive items were correctly identified)
False positive rate measures the speed of incorrect predictions
True negative rate measures the model's training time
Both rates are identical and interchangeable

explanation.correct: FPR = FP/(FP+TN) measures incorrect positive predictions among actual negatives. TNR = TN/(FP+TN) measures correct negative predictions among actual negatives. They are complementary (FPR + TNR = 1).

distractors.C: FPR measures error proportion, not speed. It's the fraction of negatives incorrectly classified.
distractors.D: TNR measures prediction quality, not training duration.
distractors.E: They are complementary (sum to 1), not identical. FPR measures errors; TNR measures correctness.

questions.stem: How can AWS hosted AI services be customized to meet specific business needs? (Select TWO)

options.text: Amazon Comprehend allows creating a custom classifier that uses your own categories by supplying training data
Many hosted services are pay-as-you-go, making them easy to evaluate before committing
Hosted services cannot be customized at all
Customization requires training from scratch
You must replace the hosted service with SageMaker for any customization

explanation.correct: Amazon Comprehend lets you create custom classifiers with your own categories and training data. Hosted services are pay-as-you-go, making it sensible to evaluate them for meeting business goals before more complex approaches.

distractors.C: Many hosted services allow customization (custom classifiers, custom categories) while remaining fully managed.
distractors.D: Customization of hosted services uses your data within the service. You don't train a model from scratch.
distractors.E: Hosted services can be customized directly. SageMaker is only needed if hosted services don't meet objectives.

questions.stem: How can SageMaker Pipelines be defined and what features do they support? (Select TWO)

options.text: Pipelines can be created using the SageMaker SDK for Python or defined using JSON
Pipelines support conditional branches based on the output of a previous step
Pipelines can only contain a single step
Pipelines cannot be viewed or visualized
Pipelines require manual execution of each step

explanation.correct: Pipelines can be created with the SageMaker SDK for Python or defined in JSON. They support conditional branches based on previous step outputs, and can be viewed in SageMaker Studio.

distractors.C: Pipelines contain all steps to build and deploy a model — multiple steps connected in a workflow.
distractors.D: Pipelines can be viewed and visualized in SageMaker Studio with a graphical representation.
distractors.E: Pipelines automate execution. That's their purpose — orchestrating steps without manual intervention.

questions.stem: How should model re-training be scheduled in production? (Select TWO)

options.text: A simple scheduled approach (daily, weekly, or monthly) is usually sufficient for most ML models
The monitoring system should detect drift and automatically initiate re-training cycles through alerts
Models never need re-training after initial deployment
Re-training should only happen when customers complain
Re-training should happen every minute for maximum freshness

explanation.correct: A scheduled approach (daily/weekly/monthly) usually suffices. Additionally, the monitoring system should detect drift, initiate alerts, and trigger re-training cycles when issues are found.

distractors.C: All models degrade over time due to drift. Re-training is essential for maintaining performance.
distractors.D: Proactive monitoring catches issues before customers notice. Reactive approaches miss early degradation.
distractors.E: Minute-by-minute retraining is excessive and wasteful. Scheduled + event-driven approaches are practical.

questions.stem: What should be considered when evaluating the business risks of an ML project? (Select TWO)

options.text: Estimate the risks of using AI and potential costs incurred from errors (e.g., loss of sales or customers)
Compare actual model operating costs with the initial cost-benefit model to calculate ROI
AI projects have zero risk because models always make correct predictions
Risk assessment is only needed for models over 1 billion parameters
Business risk doesn't apply to ML — only technical accuracy matters

explanation.correct: Estimate AI risks and potential error costs (lost sales, lost customers). After deployment, compare actual operating costs with the original cost-benefit analysis to calculate return on investment.

distractors.C: ML models are probabilistic and make errors. Risk assessment is essential because errors can be costly.
distractors.D: All ML models carry risk regardless of size. Risk assessment applies to any AI deployment.
distractors.E: Business risk is critical. ML projects must be justified by business value, not just technical performance.

questions.scenario: A data scientist receives a new dataset for model training. Before doing any transformation or feature engineering, they want to understand the data's distribution, identify outliers, and spot patterns visually.
questions.stem: What should they perform first?

options.text: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) using visualization tools to understand data characteristics before preparation
Immediately start training the model on raw data
Deploy the data to a production endpoint
Delete all outliers without understanding them

explanation.correct: EDA with visualization tools should come first to gain understanding of data distributions, outliers, and patterns. This informs how to prepare, clean, and engineer features before model building.

distractors.B: Training on raw data without understanding it leads to poor models. EDA first reveals issues to address.
distractors.C: Data isn't deployed. It needs understanding (EDA) then preparation before training.
distractors.D: Outliers need investigation, not blind deletion. EDA helps understand whether outliers are errors or valid data.

questions.scenario: A company collects IoT sensor data continuously from factory equipment. They need this data for training a predictive maintenance model. New readings arrive every second.
questions.stem: What type of data ingestion do they need for their ETL pipeline?

options.text: Streaming data ingestion — the data flows continuously in real time and can be collected via services like Amazon Kinesis or Amazon MSK
Monthly batch loading since sensor data doesn't change
Manual CSV upload once per year
No ETL is needed for sensor data

explanation.correct: With readings every second, this is streaming data requiring real-time ingestion. AWS Glue can extract from streaming services like Amazon Kinesis and Amazon MSK for continuous data collection.

distractors.B: Sensor data arrives continuously (every second). Monthly batch would miss 99.99% of the data.
distractors.C: Manual yearly upload cannot capture real-time continuous sensor streams.
distractors.D: All data needs ETL to be cleaned, transformed, and stored appropriately for ML training.

questions.scenario: An MLOps team manages 20 different models across production. They need a centralized place to track model versions, history, and manage which versions are deployed.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker capability provides centralized model version management?

options.text: SageMaker Model Registry — centralized repository for trained models and their history
SageMaker Feature Store — centralized feature management
SageMaker Ground Truth — data labeling workflows
SageMaker Canvas — low-code data preparation

explanation.correct: SageMaker Model Registry provides a centralized repository for trained models and their version history. It's the model versioning component alongside Feature Store (features) and Pipelines (orchestration).

distractors.B: Feature Store manages features/metadata. Model Registry manages trained model versions.
distractors.C: Ground Truth handles labeling. Model Registry tracks trained model versions.
distractors.D: Canvas handles data preparation. Model Registry stores and versions completed models.

questions.scenario: A fraud detection model uses a threshold of 0.5. The team notices too many legitimate transactions are being flagged (high false positive rate). They want to reduce false alarms while accepting they might miss some fraud.
questions.stem: How should they adjust the threshold?

options.text: Increase the threshold (e.g., to 0.7) — the model will require higher confidence before flagging fraud, reducing false positives but potentially missing some actual fraud (more false negatives)
Decrease the threshold to 0.3 to catch more fraud
Remove the threshold entirely
Set the threshold to exactly 1.0

explanation.correct: Increasing the threshold means the model needs higher confidence before predicting positive (fraud). This reduces false positives (fewer false alarms) but increases false negatives (some actual fraud is missed).

distractors.B: Decreasing threshold would catch more fraud but also increase false positives — the opposite of what they want.
distractors.C: A threshold is needed to convert probabilities into binary decisions. Without it, no classification happens.
distractors.D: A threshold of 1.0 means nothing would ever be classified as fraud (requires 100% certainty), which is too extreme.

questions.scenario: A data scientist evaluates two classification models. Model A has an AUC of 0.92 and Model B has an AUC of 0.54.
questions.stem: What do these AUC scores indicate about model quality?

options.text: Model A performs excellently (near perfect). Model B performs barely better than random chance (0.5 = random classifier)
Both models are equally good because both are above 0.5
Model B is better because its AUC is closer to 0.5
AUC scores cannot be compared between models

explanation.correct: AUC of 1.0 = perfect accuracy; 0.5 = no better than random. Model A (0.92) performs excellently. Model B (0.54) is barely better than flipping a coin.

distractors.B: AUC above 0.5 doesn't mean good. Model B at 0.54 is only slightly better than random — essentially useless.
distractors.C: Higher AUC is better, not lower. 0.5 is the baseline for random prediction. Being close to it means poor performance.
distractors.D: AUC is specifically designed for comparing classifiers. Higher AUC = better aggregate performance across thresholds.

questions.scenario: A data team needs to apply the same cleaning transformations to new datasets that arrive weekly. They don't want to manually recreate the transformations each time.
questions.stem: How does AWS Glue DataBrew support reusable data preparation? (Select TWO)

options.text: Save transformation steps as a recipe that can be reused with other datasets
Deploy recipes on a continuing basis for automated ongoing data preparation
Recipes automatically train ML models on the transformed data
Recipes can only be used once and must be recreated
Recipes require writing custom code for each transformation

explanation.correct: DataBrew lets you save transformation steps in a recipe for reuse with other datasets. Recipes can be updated, reused, and deployed on a continuing basis for automated, repeatable data preparation.

distractors.C: Recipes handle data preparation only. Training is a separate pipeline step.
distractors.D: Recipes are specifically designed for reuse. They can be applied to new datasets repeatedly.
distractors.E: DataBrew's 250+ transformations are point-and-click (no code). Recipes capture these visual steps.

questions.scenario: A team needs to label 1 million images. They want to minimize manual labeling effort while maintaining quality.
questions.stem: How does SageMaker Ground Truth's active learning reduce labeling effort? (Select TWO)

options.text: It uses an ML model to automatically label data that it can label with high confidence
Only data that the ML model cannot confidently label is sent to human workers
It eliminates the need for any human labeling entirely
It requires humans to label every single image regardless
Active learning only works for text data, not images

explanation.correct: Ground Truth active learning uses an ML model to automatically label what it can with confidence. Only remaining uncertain items go to human workers (Mechanical Turk or private workforce), dramatically reducing manual effort.

distractors.C: Humans are still needed for items the ML model can't confidently label. Active learning reduces but doesn't eliminate human work.
distractors.D: The point of active learning is avoiding human labeling for every item. The ML model handles the easy ones.
distractors.E: Active learning works for images, text, and other data types. It's not limited to text.

questions.scenario: An MLOps architect is designing the version control strategy for their ML pipeline. They need to identify what should be stored in centralized repositories.
questions.stem: Which SageMaker repositories support MLOps version control? (Select TWO)

options.text: SageMaker Feature Store — repository for feature definitions of training data
SageMaker Model Registry — centralized repository for trained models and version history
Amazon S3 — the only repository needed for all ML artifacts
Amazon CloudWatch — repository for model versions
AWS IAM — repository for algorithm code

explanation.correct: Feature Store is a repository for feature definitions. Model Registry is a centralized repository for trained models and history. Together with code repos and Pipelines, they form the MLOps versioning foundation.

distractors.C: S3 stores artifacts, but Feature Store and Model Registry provide the structured versioning, discovery, and management capabilities needed for MLOps.
distractors.D: CloudWatch monitors metrics and logs. It's not a model versioning repository.
distractors.E: IAM manages permissions. Code repositories (CodeCommit, GitHub) store algorithm code.

questions.scenario: A model achieves 95% accuracy on a test set where 95% of samples belong to class A and only 5% to class B. A colleague is skeptical about this impressive-sounding number.
questions.stem: Why might the 95% accuracy be misleading in this case?

options.text: The model could achieve 95% accuracy by simply predicting class A for every input, never correctly identifying class B — accuracy is misleading with imbalanced datasets
95% accuracy is always excellent regardless of data distribution
Accuracy can never reach 95% on real data
The model must be overfitting because the score is too high

explanation.correct: With 95% class A in the data, a model predicting everything as class A gets 95% accuracy while never detecting class B. This is why accuracy is not a good metric for imbalanced datasets — use precision, recall, or F1 instead.

distractors.B: High accuracy is meaningless with imbalance. A trivial classifier can achieve it by always predicting the majority class.
distractors.C: 95% accuracy is achievable but doesn't prove the model is useful when data is heavily skewed.
distractors.D: High accuracy on imbalanced data might indicate a lazy model (predicting majority class), not necessarily overfitting.

questions.scenario: A company deploys an AI-powered recommendation engine. After 3 months, they notice customer complaints have increased and some customers have left. The team needs to quantify the impact.
questions.stem: What business risk metric should they evaluate?

options.text: Estimate the potential costs from errors — including loss of sales and customers — and compare actual results against original business goals to assess ROI
Only look at model accuracy metrics
Count the number of training epochs used
Measure the model's inference latency

explanation.correct: Business risk includes potential costs from errors (lost sales, lost customers). Compare actual results with original goals and compute ROI by weighing model costs against business value gained (or lost).

distractors.B: Accuracy alone doesn't capture customer loss. Business metrics (churn, revenue impact) are needed.
distractors.C: Training epochs are development metrics, not business impact measures.
distractors.D: Latency is a technical performance metric. Customer loss requires business impact analysis.
